core_intuitions: The single-vector information bottleneck: CLIP/SigLIP crush an entire dense page (dozens of distinct facts) into ONE vector, so a specific query cannot latch onto the one fact that answers it. This failure is the whole motivation for late interaction.

The retrieval-interaction spectrum: cross-encoder (joint attention, maximally expressive, too slow to scan) vs bi-encoder (one vector per side, fast but lossy) vs late interaction (token/patch vectors + cheap MaxSim at the end) as the middle path that keeps granularity AND precomputability.

MaxSim as an operation, not a formula: each query token scans ALL document patches, keeps only its single best match (row-max of the query-by-patch similarity grid), and the page score is the sum of those best matches. There is NO query-page cross-attention; the only interaction is these dot products at the very end.

ColPali's one clean move: replace ColBERT's BERT-token document encoder with a VLM that turns a page IMAGE into a grid of ~1024 contextualized patch vectors, so a query token for 'revenue' can match the patch rendering 'Revenue: 4.2B' with no OCR ever run.

The multi-vector storage blowup (1000x) and the staged compression funnel: 1024 vectors/page instead of 1 forces token-pooling, quantization/PLAID residuals, approximate candidate generation (HNSW-over-patches / MUVERA), then exact-MaxSim rerank on a small shortlist.

Why 'no OCR' is a feature: a chart's meaning is bar heights, a table's meaning is 2-D structure; linearized OCR discards exactly that, while the page-as-image path preserves layout and hands it to a VLM.

existing.name: colpali-late-interaction-maxsim
existing.issue: Renders correctly and legibly in both themes and cleanly conveys the ColPali ENCODING pipeline (both towers -> patch vectors + query vectors -> MaxSim -> score). Its one genuine weakness is that the MaxSim box states the operator only as a formula and never shows the actual max/sum mechanism, so the 'each query token goes shopping' intuition is asserted, not visualized. Rather than overload this pipeline diagram, that gap is best filled by a dedicated operator figure (see new 'maxsim-scoring-grid'), leaving this one as the clean end-to-end encoding view it already is.
existing.improvement: Keep as-is. Optional micro-declutter: move the 'late interaction - no cross-attention between query and page' line out of the MaxSim box into a small caption below it so the box holds only the operator name + formula. Not required.

existing.name: colpali-ocrfree-rag-pipeline
existing.issue: Strong, correct, and legible in both themes. Clearly splits offline indexing vs online query, shows token-pool+quantize, the multi-vector index feeding candidate-gen + exact rerank into MaxSim retrieval, top-k PAGE IMAGES (raw pixels, no OCR) flowing to the VLM generator, and the dashed 'question also passed to VLM' line. This is the OCR-free RAG loop done well.

new_figures.name: retrieval-interaction-spectrum
new_figures.concept: Cross-encoder vs bi-encoder vs late interaction, side by side, showing why late interaction is the middle path that keeps token granularity while staying precomputable.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter explicitly frames ColBERT as sitting 'between two extremes' and the single-vector 'information bottleneck' as the reason late interaction exists, but no figure makes this three-way placement concrete. This is the foundational conceptual scaffold for the entire chapter: understand it and MaxSim/ColPali follow naturally. It also embeds the information-bottleneck intuition in the bi-encoder panel.
new_figures.placement: Section 'The ColBERT Idea', right after the paragraph contrasting cross-encoder and bi-encoder (before the MaxSim formula, around line 78-80).
new_figures.brief: Three equal-width panels sharing a common toy query Q (3-4 tiny token chips, e.g. 'Q3','revenue','growth') and a common toy document D (represented as a small page holding a few distinct fact icons: a table cell, a mini bar-chart, a footnote line). LEFT panel 'Cross-encoder': Q chips and D content fed into ONE shared stack with dense all-to-all connection lines (a small attention mesh) between every query and doc element, emerging as a single score. Sub-label: 'joint attention over Q+D'. Cost tag row: expressiveness = high, scannability = low, with the note 'O(queries x docs) forward passes - too slow to scan a corpus'. MIDDLE panel 'Bi-encoder (single vector)': Q -> encoder -> ONE vector chip; D (its several fact icons) -> encoder -> ONE vector chip, visually showing the many facts being funneled/crushed into that single chip (a small 'squeeze' motif); one dot product -> score. Sub-label: 'one vector per side'. Cost tag: expressiveness = low, scannability = high, note 'fast, fully precomputable - fine-grained detail lost (information bottleneck)'. RIGHT panel 'Late interaction (ColBERT / ColPali)' - visually marked as the chosen path (a subtle check/anchor accent border): Q -> n token vectors (a small column of chips); D -> m token/patch vectors (a slightly longer column of chips); thin links drawn only from each query chip to its single best doc chip (foreshadowing MaxSim), summing to a score. Sub-label: 'token/patch granularity kept; only cheap dot products interact, at the very end'. Cost tag: expressiveness = high, scannability = high, note 'docs precomputable AND fine-grained'. Optional bottom strip: a 2-axis mini-map (x = scannability/corpus-scale, y = expressiveness/granularity) with the three approaches plotted - cross-encoder top-left, bi-encoder bottom-right, late interaction top-right (best of both), reinforcing 'middle path = best of both'. Theme-safe: neutral strokes and fills throughout; reserve ONE accent hue exclusively for the highlighted late-interaction panel and its links. No numbers needed beyond the token labels. Everything must be a fully legible static frame; ensure the three panels and their cost tags do not overlap and read left-to-right.

new_figures.name: maxsim-scoring-grid
new_figures.concept: The MaxSim operator as a concrete grid computation: query-token x patch similarity matrix, row-maxes highlighted (each query token's single best patch), summed into the page score.
new_figures.rationale: MaxSim is THE core mechanism of the chapter and currently appears only as a formula inside a pipeline box. Turning it into a visible grid-with-row-maxes-then-sum is what makes the 'each query term goes shopping across the whole document for its best evidence' metaphor click. A single static frame fully conveys it, which is exactly the disciplined choice over animation.
new_figures.placement: Section 'The ColBERT Idea', immediately after the MaxSim formula and the toy maxsim() code block (around line 105-107), where MaxSim is first defined.
new_figures.brief: Left region: a small document-page thumbnail with a coarse patch grid overlaid (e.g. 4 rows x 5 cols). Shade two or three specific patches to suggest content - one patch on a table cell that (in a callout) renders 'Revenue: 4.2B', one on a small chart. These patches are the columns of the matrix. CENTER region: the similarity matrix. ROWS = query tokens (label 3-4, e.g. 'Q3','revenue','growth','?'). COLUMNS = document patches (show ~8 columns with an ellipsis for the remaining ~1016, since a real page has ~1024). Fill each cell with a single-hue sequential intensity encoding similarity magnitude (theme-safe: intensity of ONE accent over a neutral base, works in light and dark). In EACH row, outline/box the single highest-intensity cell = that query token's best-matching patch; draw a faint connector from a boxed cell to its patch on the page thumbnail (especially the 'revenue' row -> the 'Revenue: 4.2B' table-cell patch, with the annotation 'the token "revenue" finds the patch that renders the revenue figure - no OCR run'). RIGHT region: pull each row's max value into a small vertical list of illustrative decimals (use small values like 0.9, 0.4, 0.7, 0.6 - illustrative only, NOT benchmark numbers), then a large Sigma summing them to the final score S(Q,D). Baked-in caption line: 'Each query token scans ALL patches and keeps only its single best match (row max); the page score is the sum of those best matches. The only query-page interaction is these dot products, computed at the very end - hence "late" interaction.' Keep it uncluttered: at most 4 query rows and ~8 shown patch columns; the row-max boxes must be unambiguous; ensure the page thumbnail, matrix, and sum column sit side by side without overlap in both themes. Static frame is fully sufficient; do not require motion.

new_figures.name: multivector-storage-funnel
new_figures.concept: The 1000x storage blowup of multi-vector indexing and the staged compress-then-shortlist funnel that makes it affordable.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter's central engineering claim is that going from 1 vector/page to ~1024 vectors/page (a 1000x blowup) is what forces token pooling, quantization/PLAID, HNSW-over-patches/MUVERA, and a coarse-to-fine funnel. This lives only in a numeric worked example and a prose interview answer; a single funnel infographic makes the whole systems story of the second half of the chapter legible at a glance. Priority 2 because it is partly foreshadowed by the RAG pipeline figure and the worked example.
new_figures.placement: Section 'Indexing Multi-Vector Embeddings at Scale', at the top of the section right after the opening paragraph about avoiding the full O(pages x patches x q-tokens) scan (around line 199).
new_figures.brief: TOP BAND (the blowup): on the left, one page -> SigLIP '1 vector' shown as a single chip; on the right, the same page -> ColPali '~1024 patch vectors' shown as a dense grid of chips; a bold 'x1024' multiplier between them. Directly below, two horizontal index-size bars for a 100k-page corpus using the chapter's own illustrative figures: single-vector ~25.6 MB (a tiny bar) vs multi-vector ~25.6 GiB (a bar ~1000x longer, drawn with a break/ellipsis so it fits the viewBox), labelled 'the factor of 1024 is the price of late interaction'. MAIN BODY (the staged funnel, read left-to-right, visibly narrowing): Stage 1 'Compress at index time' - icon of the 1024-chip grid shrinking to ~128 chips (token pooling) AND bit-depth dropping (fp16 -> 1-2 bit residuals / binary quantize, PLAID); annotation 'shrinks the index ~10-30x'. Stage 2 'Approximate candidate generation' - PLAID centroids OR HNSW-over-patches OR MUVERA fixed-dim encoding; each query token hits an ANN index; output = union of a few hundred candidate pages; annotation 'never touches full-precision vectors'. Stage 3 'Exact MaxSim rerank' - decompress the FULL fp16 patches for ONLY the shortlist, compute exact MaxSim, return top 3-5 pages. Show the funnel width shrinking across stages: 100k pages -> few hundred candidates -> top 3-5. Along the bottom, depict the two-tier storage split: a 'compressed / in-RAM' tier feeding stages 1-2, and a 'full fp16 / on NVMe (memory-mapped)' tier feeding only stage 3. Use ONLY the chapter's own illustrative numbers (1, 1024, 128, ~25.6 MB, ~25.6 GiB, 'few hundred', 'top 3-5'); invent no benchmark numbers. Theme-safe single accent for the funnel flow over neutral stage boxes; ensure the top band and the funnel are clearly separated and each stage label is legible in both themes; wide bars must stay inside the viewBox (use a break glyph rather than running off-canvas). A static frame suffices.

summary: The chapter has two strong, theme-correct pipeline figures (ColPali encoding path and the OCR-free RAG loop) - both KEEP. What is missing is the conceptual and mechanistic core: (1) the cross-encoder vs bi-encoder vs late-interaction SPECTRUM that motivates the entire chapter and embeds the single-vector information bottleneck; (2) MaxSim shown as an actual grid-with-row-maxes-then-sum operation rather than a formula in a box, making the 'each query token goes shopping for its best patch' intuition click; and (3) the multi-vector storage-blowup-and-compression FUNNEL that is the engineering crux of the second half. I propose these three new static figures (spectrum and grid are priority 1, funnel priority 2). No animation is warranted - each mechanism is fully expressible in a single well-designed frame.
